item.title: How to Use Flat-Bottomed Potentials in GROMACS
item.description: Flat bottomed potentials (FBPs) are a unique (and powerful) type of restraint we can apply to specific particles in a molecular dynamics simulation. In short, we define a fixed geometric region within the simulation box to which certain atoms will either be attracted or repelled. They’re also a little tricky to understand from a technical perspective. In this post, I’ll briefly discuss the maths, how to implement an FBP in the topology and restraints files, and finish up with some practical notes for usage.

item.title: Math examples
item.description: This is an inline \(a^*=x-b^*\) equation. These are block equations: \[a^*=x-b^*\] \[ a^*=x-b^* \] \[ a^*=x-b^* \] These are block equations using alternate delimiters: $$a^*=x-b^*$$ $$ a^*=x-b^* $$ $$ a^*=x-b^* $$

Jackson Crowley Link to heading I use computers to simulate biological systems. Specifically, I’m a PhD Student in computational biophysics based in Lyon, France. I work on atomistic and coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations of lipid droplets (and more).
